Section: Annex
Text: The author of the communication is D.S., a Slovak national born in 1971. She claims that she is a victim of discrimination on the grounds of gender and her marital and family status in violation of article 2 (a), (c) and (e) read in conjunction with articles 1 and 11 (1) (a) of the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against Women. The Convention and the Optional Protocol thereto entered into force for the State party in 1993 and 2000, respectively. The author is represented by Vanda Durbakova, attorney at the Center for Civil and Human Rights.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: The author was employed by the Slovak National Library in Martin, Slovakia, as a research and development worker in the Department of Biographical Research and Creation of Biographical Dictionaries within the National Biographical Institute between 15 September 1995 and 31 March 2008. The Library is a State-run entity, funded by the Ministry of Culture.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: In December 2001, with the consent of her employer, the author went on maternity leave, which she combined with parental leave, and after having had two children she resumed work on 10 January 2008.[[fn:1]] Upon her return, she was informed by the Personnel Department that she had been ordered by the employer to take her accrued annual leave of 42 days, which she had not taken during her maternity leave, by 10 March 2008.

Footnotes.text: Slovak law provides for maternity leave of 37 weeks for one child and for parental leave for extended childcare until the child is 3 years old.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: While the author was on leave, the Slovak National Library was reorganized. At the beginning of 2008, the head of the Library decided to reduce the number of employees in the light of projected budget cuts. The author’s post was abolished.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: On 20 February 2008, the Director of the National Biographical Institute, the author’s head of department, notified her by telephone to immediately come to the workplace. When she arrived, the Director of the Institute told her that she was dismissed as from 1 March 2008, because the department had to dismiss one person. During the conversation, the Director stated that the decision to dismiss the author was partially due to the fact that she had two small children and had just returned from leave, and furthermore that she was the only one who was nobody’s “protégé”. On the same day, during a meeting with the Deputy Director of the Slovak National Library, from whom the author wished to know the actual reason or reasons for her dismissal given her social situation as a breadwinner for her two minor children, the Deputy Director stated that the Library was “not a social institute”.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: The author returned to the Slovak National Library on 25 February 2008 and requested the Deputy Director of the Library to give her the decision on organizational changes, the collective agreement and “an exact wording of the dismissal”. Her request was rejected and the Deputy Director, who informed her that as from 1 March 2008 she was in any case dismissed, made dishonouring comments, which the claimant felt impinged upon her personal dignity (“I don’t poop in my pants because of you!”).

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: On 4 March 2008, the author received a notice informing her that her post would be abolished as from 1 June 2008 and that the Slovak National Library could not offer her another post that would be appropriate for her. On 10 March 2008, the author signed an agreement on the termination of her employment pursuant to sections 60 and 63 of the Labour Law Code (redundancy) and her employment was duly terminated on 31 March 2008.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: The author claims that the Director and Deputy Director of the National Biographical Institute decided to dismiss her because they doubted that she would be able to perform her job properly as a mother of two small children. They expressed concern about her ability to find a balance between work and family life. The Deputy Director also openly expressed concerns that the author would be frequently absent when her children fell ill. The author was told by colleagues that the Deputy Director had also stated this during a staff meeting.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: On 1 April 2008, another person (a pensioner) was hired by the Slovak National Library to perform duties similar to those previously performed by the author. This person has since confirmed before the Martin District Court that the work was practically the same as that undertaken by the author. Another pensioner was also hired to support this employee in substantially the same duties as those carried out previously by the author. These appointments were made even though the employer told the author that her post had been abolished.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: On 17 April 2008, the author complained to the Minister of Culture about the discriminatory treatment that she felt she had suffered during her employment termination process. On 5 May 2008, the Ministry of Culture informed her that it was not authorized to deal with personnel matters and advised her to make a complaint before a court. Her complaint was also sent to the Slovak National Library for review.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: On 15 May 2008, the Director General of the Slovak National Library sent the author a letter of apology for the conduct of the Director and Deputy Director of the National Biographical Institute during her employment termination process, noting that her supervisors’ actions should have been more sensitive. The author did not, however, receive an apology for the discriminatory treatment and neither was any investigation launched into her complaint, to which no further response was received. On 19 January 2009, the author again unsuccessfully complained to the Director General of the Library about the manner in which she had been treated.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: On 4 August 2008, the author submitted a complaint to the Martin District Court. She argued that the Slovak National Library had violated the principle of equal treatment inasmuch as the decision to declare her redundant was taken by the Director and Deputy Director of the National Biographical Institute, who respectively had told her that she was nobody’s “protégé” and that she would be at home with sick children all the time. The author also argued that, after the termination of her employment, the Library had engaged two other persons to perform the tasks that had previously been performed by her. She alleged that the main reason for her dismissal was the fact that she was a mother of two small children and had just returned from maternity and parental leave.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: On 15 April 2010, the Martin District Court dismissed her complaint as ill-founded. It stated that, because she had not sustained her burden of proof of prima facie discrimination, the burden of proof could not be shifted to the defendant. The Court stated that it could not review an employer’s reasons for designating an employee as redundant and that the fact that someone else was employed to perform the author’s duties had no bearing on her discrimination-related complaint. The Court found that the employer’s aim in reducing the budget was legitimate.

Section: Facts as submitted by the author
Text: The author appealed against the District Court’s decision to the Regional Court in Žilina, claiming that the District Court had erred in its assessment of law and fact. She claimed that it had not properly applied the burden of proof as laid down in section 11 (2) of the Anti-Discrimination Act, which shifts the burden of proof to the defendant once a prima facie case has been presented by the plaintiff. The Regional Court dismissed her appeal on 30 March 2011, having maintained the decision of the lower court. On 1 July 2011, the author complained to the Supreme Court, claiming that the Regional Court had violated her right to a fair trial, but her complaint was dismissed as manifestly ill-founded on 25 September 2012. On 10 December 2012, the author complained to the Constitutional Court, claiming that the lower courts’ decisions were arbitrary, unjustified and unsubstantiated. She also claimed a violation of her rights to a fair trial, effective remedy and nondiscrimination, as guaranteed under the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, as well as a violation of her rights under articles 2 (c) and (e) and 11 (1) (a) of the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against Women. On 6 February 2013, the Constitutional Court rejected her complaint, dismissing the author’s arguments as groundless and confirming the lower courts’ decisions.

Section: Complaint
Text: The author claims that she was subjected to a form of gender-based discrimination by her employer who terminated her labour contract on prohibited discriminatory grounds. She claims that article 2 (a), (c) and (e) read in conjunction with articles 1 and 11 (1) (a) of the Convention has been violated because the State party failed to ensure her effective protection as a woman against the gender-based discrimination that she was subjected to by the national courts and failed to take all appropriate measures to eliminate discrimination against women, inter alia, in the field of employment.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: By its note verbale of 29 September 2014, the State party transmitted its observations on admissibility and merits. The State party states that it is committed to gender equality. In support, it enumerates national legislation that gives effect to its obligations under the international human rights treaties on the matter.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: The State party further submits that it has no objection as to the admissibility of the communication.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: As to merits, the State party observes that the author may have felt disadvantaged based on her sex and marital status after having lost her employment. It claims, however, that a violation of the principle of equal treatment had not been proved in the court proceedings. The State party explains that the Slovak National Library had been objectively obliged to reduce the number of employees and increase work efficiency owing to budgetary necessity. Based on the composition of redundancies, which included 9 women and 13 men, among whom 3 were women with children and 13 were of retirement age, it could not be assumed that gender-based discrimination had occurred.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: The State party contends that the employer was able to show in court that the decision to dismiss the author was based on organizational change in relation to her work position rather than on her personal circumstances. It asserts that the Slovak National Library employs other women with minor children and also had attempted to retain the author on a part-time basis, but that she declined that offer. The State party submits that, because the author signed an agreement to terminate her employment, the termination was in fact in accordance with her will.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: As to the recruitment of a new employee who carried out some of the same work as the author, the State party refers to the Slovak National Library having shown in the court proceedings that this happened only after the decision of the author to terminate her employment by agreement during the given notice period, and that it was in any case not a permanent position but a short-term contract.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: The State party endorses the view of its courts to the effect that there was a lack of sensitivity in the way in which the matter was dealt with by the employer and therefore welcomes the written apology issued by the employer to the author. Since, however, no one else was present during the exchange between the author and her employer, the State party does not accept that it caused the author harm that could affect her professional life or reduce her dignity. Having said this, the State party concedes that the private nature of the conduct does not make it excusable.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: The State party also claims that, because the author failed to meet her burden of presenting facts before the courts that gave rise to a reasonable presumption of discrimination, the burden of proof did not shift to the defendant to prove that there had been no violation of the principle of equal treatment. The State party also avers that the author failed to specify which of her rights, exactly, were violated by the employer.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: Furthermore, on the issue of the shift of the burden of proof, the State party reiterates that this is an important tool in efforts to combat discrimination. In practice, it means that, if a plaintiff submits to a court such facts as give rise to a reasonable assumption that discrimination indeed occurred, the burden of proof in the proceedings is shifted to the defendant, who is then required to prove that there was no violation of the principle of equal treatment. The State party states that the essence of this legal instrument lies in the provision to the plaintiff of effective means of protection against discrimination by which he or she is not compelled to provide evidence of the alleged conduct. The State party, however, agrees with the view of the courts that this does not mean that the author is entirely exempted from the obligation to submit relevant facts in support of her claims. Pursuant to section 11 (2) of the Anti-Discrimination Act, the shift of the burden of proof occurs only when the court may reasonably assume, based on the facts presented by the plaintiff, that the alleged violation occurred. It is not sufficient for a person who feels discriminated against to merely indicate that. The person should not only maintain but should also demonstrate that he or she has not been treated in a usual, i.e. nondiscriminatory, way. The State party asserts that this interpretation is in line with the explanatory memorandum to the Act, which states that, in conformity with the provisions of the directive, it “activates” the plaintiff and shifts the burden of proof on to the defendant only when it is possible to assume from the evidence presented by the plaintiff that a violation of the principle of equal treatment could have actually occurred. The State party adds that these facts, by their nature, especially by the very intensity of the intervention, and by the circumstances under which they occurred, must be so serious that the court may reasonably presume discriminatory conduct by the defendant.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: The State party confirms that it had acquainted itself with the justification of the courts that the author did not sustain her burden of proof and thus the burden of proof was not shifted to the employer. The State party reiterates that the author did not present facts that would give rise to a reasonable presumption that discriminatory conduct by the employer occurred. It further states that the author failed to specify which of her specific rights had been affected by the employer’s conduct.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: The State party does not accept the contention by the author that she had been requested to prove before the courts facts such as the motivation of the employer, given that, the State party claims, this is presumed in cases of unequal treatment. It also refutes the author’s claim that the national courts incorrectly assessed matters both of fact and law. It considers that the author had ordinary and extraordinary remedies available and that she used them actively. It states that neither the general courts nor the constitutional courts, however, reached the conclusion that there had been a violation of the author’s rights.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: The State party asserts that the author had access to fair national processes, which she had availed herself of and yet had been unsuccessful. It states that the right to a fair trial includes the right of a party to the reasoning of the judicial decision that clearly and unambiguously provides answers to all the relevant legal and factual issues relating to judicial protection. It observes, however, that the courts do not have an obligation to answer all the questions raised by a party. The obligation extends only to answering those questions essential to the cause or to sufficiently explaining the actual and legal basis for the decision, without going into all the details provided by the parties. Reasons for the decision that clearly explain the basis on which it was made are sufficient to ensure that fair trial standards have been observed.

Section: State party’s observations on admissibility and merits
Text: The State party concludes that the national courts dealt with the author’s claim in accordance with the purpose and meaning of the anti-discrimination legislation. That she was not granted protection does not indicate a violation of her right to an effective remedy. It states that general courts cannot violate the right to an effective remedy if they act in accordance with procedural rules.[[fn:2]] Thus, the State party considers that there was no violation of the author’s rights under the Convention.

Footnotes.text: The State party also adds that the author had the opportunity to seek a remedy before the European Court of Human Rights, which she did not avail herself of.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: On 1 December 2014, the author submitted her comments on the State party’s observations. She reiterates that the termination of her work contract violated the principle of equal treatment under national legislation.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: She states that the termination caused her to suffer psychological pressure and humiliation. She underlines the fact that her superiors referred explicitly in conversation to the fact that having children and having just finished parental leave were the reasons for the termination of her contract. Her employer also made other inappropriate comments, said that the Slovak National Library was not a social institute and expressed doubts about her ability to balance work and family life. According to the author, this clearly indicates that sex and marital status played a key role in her dismissal. She asserts that organizational change was used as a pretext to hide the discriminatory nature of her dismissal.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: The author reiterates that only she among those who were dismissed had small children and had just finished parental leave, contrary to the State party’s submission. The author suggests, however, that, if, as claimed by the State, other mothers in other departments were also terminated, this does not exclude discriminatory conduct but rather strengthens the indication of discrimination.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: The author refutes the State party’s submission that her termination was at her initiative and in accordance with her will, arguing that the termination of the contract was made solely at the initiative of her employer. Termination was presented as the only option and she therefore signed the agreement.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: She reiterates her previous statement to the effect that employing others to do her job after terminating her employment served only to highlight that her workload was not terminated, which had been the claim of her employer in justifying her termination. She states that this is a clear violation of her rights under national antidiscrimination legislation, but it was ignored by the national courts.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: She rejects the State party’s contention that she was offered a part-time work agreement by the employer, which was confirmed in testimony by her Director during the court hearing on 11 February 2010. She states, however, that any such unilateral change in her conditions of work would in any case have violated her right to equal treatment.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: The author asserts that the courts’ failure to effectively implement national antidiscrimination legislation by recognizing discriminatory treatment and providing her with an effective remedy caused her pecuniary damage in the form of legal fees, given that she was ordered to pay the court fees.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: As to the State party’s interpretation of the burden of proof under antidiscrimination legislation, the author notes that the State party’s position is in violation of relevant European Union directives and the jurisprudence of international bodies, including the European Court of Human Rights, the Court of Justice of the European Union and the United Nations treaty bodies. The author confirms that she had presented a prima facie case of discrimination to the courts and therefore the burden of proof should have shifted to the employer as the defendant. The courts failed to effectively implement this, which was compounded by the State party’s unwillingness to identify and remedy that failure.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: The author states that the need to identify and sanction gender-based discrimination in employment is important not only to provide her with a remedy but also as a preventive measure to ensure that such acts do not happen in the future. She reiterates that her case should be seen in the broader context of traditional sex-role stereotyping in the labour market, which is still widespread in Slovakia. She notes the State party’s contention that measures have been adopted to prevent discrimination, but maintains that the high incidence of discrimination indicates that the measures are ineffective or insufficient.

Section: Author’s comments on the State party’s observations
Text: The author therefore claims that the State party failed to rebut her assertions and therefore reasserts the violation of her rights under article 2 (a), (c) and (e) read in conjunction with articles 1 and 11 (1) (a) of the Convention.

Section: Issues and proceedings before the Committee concerning admissibility
Text: In accordance with rule 64 of its rules of procedure, the Committee must decide whether the communication is admissible under the Optional Protocol. Pursuant to rule 72 (4), it is to do so before considering the merits of the communication.

Section: Issues and proceedings before the Committee concerning admissibility
Text: The Committee notes that the State party has raised no objections concerning the admissibility of the author’s submission. Having found no obstacles to the admissibility of the author’s claims under article 2 (a), (c) and (e) read in conjunction with articles 1 and 11 (1) (a) of the Convention, the Committee finds them admissible and proceeds to their consideration on the merits.

Section: Consideration of the merits
Text: The Committee has considered the present communication in the light of all the information presented to it by the author and by the State party, in accordance with the provisions of article 7 (1) of the Optional Protocol.

Section: Consideration of the merits
Text: The Committee notes the author’s claims that she was dismissed on discriminatory grounds as a woman and a mother of two minor children who had just returned from maternity and parental leave; that the employer’s purported reason for dismissing her, linked to budgetary cuts, was false, given that two other persons were engaged to perform her duties after her employment was terminated; and that the national courts failed to properly apply the Act on Equal Treatment in Certain Areas and Protection against Discrimination and the Anti-Discrimination Act when they failed to shift the burden of proof to the employer, even though she had made a prima facie case of discrimination based on sex and marital and family status. The Committee observes that the author’s claims, as submitted to the Committee, have been considered by the national courts and, therefore, relate in essence to an evaluation of facts and evidence by those courts. The Committee recalls that it does not replace the national authorities in the assessment of the facts and evidence, unless such assessment was clearly arbitrary or biased due to sex or gender stereotypes or prejudice or amounted to a denial of justice. The Committee thus has to establish whether the national judicial proceedings in the author’s case were arbitrary and/or amounted to a denial of justice.

Section: Consideration of the merits
Text: In this context, the Committee notes that, pursuant to section 11 (2) of the Anti-Discrimination Act, the burden of proof is shifted to the defendant and the latter has the obligation to prove that there was no violation of the principle of equal treatment if the facts submitted to the court by the plaintiff give rise to a “reasonable assumption” that a violation of the principle of equal treatment has occurred. The Committee takes note of the contention of the State party that the court is to cause the burden of proof to be shifted only when it may reasonably assume, based on the facts presented by the plaintiff, that the alleged violation occurred and that these facts, by their nature, the intensity of the intervention and the circumstances under which they occurred, must be so serious as to lead the court to such an assumption. The Committee has given due consideration to the submission of the State party that the budget cut was a legitimate reason to abolish the author’s post and that the employer also abolished the posts occupied by 9 other women and 13 men, among whom 3 were women with children and 13 were of retirement age, which therefore excluded the presumption of any discrimination. The Committee also takes note of the State party’s contention that two persons were hired to perform the author’s tasks only after she had left and that their contract was of a temporary nature.

Section: Consideration of the merits
Text: In the present case, the Committee observes that the author presented before the national courts the same relevant facts presented before it in support of her claim, namely that: (a) organizational changes happened during the time that she was forced to take her accrued leave of 42 days; (b) she was dismissed from her employment immediately after she returned from her maternity and parental leave; (c) the news of her dismissal was given to her by the head of her department, the Director of the National Biographical Institute; (d) she was informed that she would be dismissed because the Institute had to dismiss one person and she was the only one who was nobody’s “protégé”; (e) the Deputy Director of the Slovak National Library refused to give her details about the organizational changes, the collective agreement and the exact wording of her dismissal and made very condescending remarks to her; and (f) the Deputy Director of the Institute commented that she would be at home with sick children all the time and the actual reason for her dismissal as mentioned by her superiors was the fact that she had two small children who would often fall ill and she would not be able to combine her family and professional life. The author also presented unchallenged evidence to the effect that she received a letter of apology from the Director General of the Library concerning the conduct and insensitive treatment meted out to her by her superiors, the Director and Deputy Director of the Institute, as well as of the fact that two new persons were hired after her dismissal to perform her duties. The Committee notes that the national courts dismissed the first argument of the author on the basis that it was up to the employer to decide which positions to cut and that this decision was based on a legitimate aim of reducing the budget, although the workforce after the redundancy amounted to 270 persons. The second argument put across by the author was dismissed on the ground of the personal nature of the conversation of the author with her superiors, which constituted only some interference with the author’s “personal sphere” not so intense as to damage her professional life. The other arguments of the author were simply dismissed as irrelevant to her claim of discriminatory treatment.

Section: Consideration of the merits
Text: The Committee considers that, even if the budget cut may have been considered a legitimate reason to abolish the post occupied by the author, the timing of her dismissal; the manner in which she was informed about her dismissal; the refusal to give her any details about the organizational change, the collective agreement and the exact wording of her dismissal; and the remarks of her superiors, which clearly indicated discriminatory treatment against her and were of such a serious nature as to result in a letter of apology sent to her by no less than the Director General of the Slovak National Library, in addition to the fact that two other persons were hired to perform her job after her employment was terminated, called for a closer scrutiny by the national courts, more particularly the court of first instance, the Martin District Court, of the actual reasons underlying the author’s dismissal and her allegations of discrimination and violation of the principle of equal treatment. The Committee also observes that the District Court paid no heed to the fact that the two persons hired in 2008 to perform the author’s tasks, allegedly on a temporary basis, were still employed at the time of the court proceedings in 2010, i.e. two years after the author’s employment had been terminated. The Committee is of the view that no regard was paid to the failure by the employer to provide any satisfactory explanation regarding the alleged budgetary cut as the reason for the author’s dismissal. The Committee is of the view that the national courts gave a narrow interpretation to section 11 (2) of the Anti-Discrimination Act when they failed to reverse the burden of proof. The Committee is also of the view that, from the evidence on record, there was more than a “reasonable assumption” that the principle of equal treatment had been violated. The Committee further notes that the courts’ consecutive failures to effectively implement the anti-discrimination legislation, by reversing the burden of proof in favour of the author upon presentation of a prima facie case of discriminatory treatment by the employer and his préposé/s, constitute a violation of her right to an effective remedy and have the effect of denying her the possibility of appropriate satisfaction and reparation for damage suffered. In the light of the foregoing, the Committee considers that the arguments presented by the author before the national courts were sufficient to make a prima facie claim of discrimination and that requesting additional proof of discriminatory behaviour by the employer placed a disproportionate burden on the author. In the light of the information made available to it, the Committee concludes that, by not shifting the burden of proof to the defendant, the State party violated the author’s rights under article 2 (a), (c) and (e) read in conjunction with articles 1 and 11 (1) (a) of the Convention.

Section: Consideration of the merits
Text: Acting under article 7 (3) of the Optional Protocol, and in the light of the above considerations, the Committee is of the view that the State party has failed to fulfil its obligations under article 2 (a), (c) and (e) read in conjunction with articles 1 and 11 (1) (a) of the Convention and recommends that the State party provide the author with an effective remedy:

Section: (b) General:
Text: In accordance with article 7 (4) of the Optional Protocol, the State party shall give due consideration to the views of the Committee, together with its recommendations, and shall submit to the Committee, within six months, a written response, including information on any action taken in the light of the views and recommendations of the Committee. The State party is requested to have the Committee’s views and recommendations translated into Slovak, to publish them and to have them widely disseminated, in order to reach all sectors of society.
